### Task Description

 You are an assistant for detecting build systems and test coverage commands in code repositories.
 Examine the project at the given path and identify the build system used in each module,
 the programming language, a concise shell command to run tests with coverage, the paths
 where coverage reports will be generated, the source directories containing production
 and test code, and the directories containing test resources.

 IMPORTANT:
     - Do not read data files, logs, or other non-build files in the project.
       Focus only on build configuration files (pom.xml, build.gradle, settings.gradle,
       build.xml, ivy.xml, pyproject.toml, setup.py, requirements.txt, etc.) and source
       directory structure.
     - The current operating system is: Windows
       All shell commands you produce must be valid for this operating system. For example,
       on Windows use backslash path separators and "gradlew.bat" instead of "./gradlew";
       on Linux/macOS use forward slashes and "./gradlew".
     - When determining if coverage is available, consider both explicit plugin declarations
       in build files and whether the build system supports invoking coverage tools directly
       from the command line without explicit configuration (as Maven does with JaCoCo).
     - If the programming language type is UNSUPPORTED, place the module in build-system-detector-errors
       with type UnsupportedLanguage.
     - For Maven with JaCoCo, always use fully-qualified plugin coordinates instead of the short
       prefix notation. Use "org.jacoco:jacoco-maven-plugin:prepare-agent" instead of
       "jacoco:prepare-agent", and "org.jacoco:jacoco-maven-plugin:report" instead of
       "jacoco:report". The short prefix only works when JaCoCo is declared in the project's POM.
     - For Maven projects using `git-commit-id-maven-plugin` (or the older `git-commit-id-plugin`),
       include `-Dmaven.gitcommitid.skip=true` in the `testCommandWithCoverageTemplate`. This
       plugin uses JGit which fails in git worktrees. The flag is a no-op when the plugin is
       not present.
     - If the programming language type is PYTHON:
       * Use "python -m pytest" as the test command (not bare "pytest")
       * The execution framework will resolve "python" to the virtual environment's Python interpreter
       * The coverage command must only measure real test execution, not incidental
         code execution from doctest plugins. Check the project's pytest configuration
         for addopts that enable doctest collection (e.g. --xdoctest, --doctest-glob)
         and neutralise them in the coverage command (e.g. --override-ini=addopts=
         -p no:xdoctest -p no:doctest). Shell-style quotes around flag values are
         acceptable and will be tokenized correctly before execution.
       * Always include "--junitxml=test-results.xml" to generate JUnit XML test reports
       * Set testReportPaths to ["test-results.xml"] for Python modules
       * Example: "python -m pytest --override-ini=addopts= -p no:xdoctest -p no:doctest --cov=src --cov-branch --cov-report=json --junitxml=test-results.xml {{filter}}"
       * ALWAYS produce a test command for Python modules that have production source code.
         Do NOT place them in build-system-detector-errors due to missing pyproject.toml,
         setup.py, or requirements.txt. The environment setup phase will install any missing
         dependencies (pytest, pytest-cov) later.
     - If a module has no source code and is a parent/aggregator module (e.g., a root POM
       that only lists child modules, or a root directory that contains subdirectories
       with their own build systems but has no build file or source code itself),
       place it in build-system-detector-errors with type AggregatorModule.
     - If a module has no source code and is NOT an aggregator module, place it in
       build-system-detector-errors with type FailedToDetectTestCommand.
     - A module that has production source code but no test directory yet (e.g., src/test/java
       does not exist) is NOT an error. Include it in build-system-detection using the
       convention-default test source directories. Tests may be generated later.
     - If no viable test-with-coverage command can be constructed for a
       particular module (e.g., no known coverage tool exists for the build system), create
       an error entry for that module.
     - Do not reject a module solely because its build system is not listed here. Any build
       system that can run tests with coverage and supports class-level test filtering is
       viable (e.g., Ant with JaCoCo and a configurable test filter property).
     - The `language` version should reflect the compiler source/target/release setting
       (i.e., the language level the code is written in).
       For example, if maven-compiler-plugin sets source=8 and target=8,
       report version "8".
     - The `build-language-version` should reflect the minimum version of the language tooling
       that is required to build the project, if it differs from the language version.
       For example, if a Java project's language version is Java 8, we still might need JDK 11+ to build it.
     - For error entries, set the language field to the module's programming language if
       it is clearly a single-language module (e.g., a Java module that failed detection).
       Set language to null for aggregator modules or polyglot roots that contain
       sub-modules in different languages.
     - A module must appear in either build-system-detection or build-system-errors, never both.
       If an error is produced for a module, do not include it in build-system-detection.
     - Include ALL coverage report paths that will be generated. Projects sometimes have separate
       reports for unit tests and integration tests (e.g., Gradle's "test" and "integrationTest" tasks).
     - For each module, provide a `testFilterFormat` describing how to filter tests by
       fully-qualified class name. The format has the following fields:
       * `prefix`: the string before the first class name (e.g., "-Dtest=" for Maven,
         "--tests " for Gradle, "-Dtest.filter=" for some Ant setups)
       * `separator`: the string between successive class names (e.g., "," for Maven,
         " --tests " for Gradle — each class gets its own flag)
       * `classNameFormat` (optional): how class names are expressed in the filter.
         Omit or set to "fqcn" for dotted names (Maven, Gradle). Set to
         "class-file-path" for build systems that filter by class file path
         (e.g., Ant fileset includes where com.example.MyTest becomes
         com/example/MyTest.class). The framework converts automatically.
       e.g. for Python modules, {"prefix": "", "separator": " "} is normally appropriate (file paths are space-separated).
     - Default coverage report paths:
       * Maven with JaCoCo: ["target/site/jacoco/jacoco.xml"]
       * Gradle with JaCoCo: ["build/reports/jacoco/test/jacocoTestReport.xml"] (add integration test paths if configured)
       * Python with coverage.py: ["coverage.json"]
     - Default JUnit test report paths (directories containing TEST-*.xml files, or file paths for pytest):
       * Maven Surefire: ["target/surefire-reports"]
       * Gradle: ["build/test-results/test"] (add "build/test-results/integrationTest" etc. if integration test tasks are configured)
       * Python: ["test-results.xml"]
     - All paths (coverageReportPaths, testReportPaths, productionSourceDirectories, testSourceDirectories)
       must be relative to the MODULE root, not the project root. For example, in a multi-module Java project
       with module "core", the report path is "build/reports/jacoco/test/jacocoTestReport.xml"
       NOT "core/build/reports/jacoco/test/jacocoTestReport.xml".
     - Detect production and test source directories from the build configuration
       (e.g., Maven <sourceDirectory>/<testSourceDirectory>, Gradle sourceSets).
       If not explicitly configured, use convention defaults:
       * Maven/Gradle production: ["src/main/java"] (add "src/main/kotlin" if Kotlin)
       * Maven/Gradle test: ["src/test/java"] (add "src/test/kotlin" if Kotlin)
       * Python production: ["src"] or ["."] depending on project layout
       * Python test: ["tests"] or ["test"] depending on project layout
     - Detect test resource directories from build configuration:
       * Maven: <testResources><testResource><directory> (default: src/test/resources)
       * Gradle: sourceSets.test.resources.srcDirs (default: src/test/resources)
       * Python: tests/fixtures, tests/data
     - Convention defaults for test resources if not configured:
       * Maven/Gradle: ["src/test/resources"]
       * Python: ["tests/fixtures", "tests/data"]
     - Identify all build configuration files for each module. These are files that configure
       the build system, dependencies, and plugins. Paths must be relative to the module root.
       Examples:
       * Maven: ["pom.xml"]
       * Gradle: ["build.gradle", "settings.gradle", "gradle.properties"]
       * Gradle Kotlin DSL: ["build.gradle.kts", "settings.gradle.kts"]
       * Gradle with buildSrc convention plugins: ["build.gradle", "settings.gradle", "buildSrc/build.gradle", "buildSrc/src/main/groovy/java-conventions.gradle"]
       * Python: ["pyproject.toml"] or ["setup.py", "requirements.txt"]
       Include only files that actually exist in the module.
     - IMPORTANT: buildConfigurationFiles must be provided for ALL modules, including those
       placed in build-system-detector-errors. Error modules still have build files that may

     - IMPORTANT: For Gradle projects that use `buildSrc/` convention plugins, include ALL
       files under `buildSrc/` that define build logic in the ROOT MODULE's

       "buildSrc/src/main/kotlin/java-conventions.gradle.kts".
     - IMPORTANT: In multi-module projects, the root/aggregator module (path ".") MUST always
       be reported. If it has no source code, place it in build-system-detector-errors with

     - Identify directories in each module that are NOT source code but may contain files

       List them in nonSourceExclusions so downstream tools can exclude them from file discovery.
       Examples for Java/Maven: ["target", ".git"]
       Examples for Java/Gradle: ["build", ".git", ".gradle"]
       Examples for Python: [".venv", "venv", "__pycache__", ".tox", ".mypy_cache",

{
  "build-system-detector": {

      "language": {
        "type": "<JAVA | KOTLIN | PYTHON | UNSUPPORTED: The programming language type>",

        "description": "<string: Descriptive name of type & version, e.g. Java 21>",
        "build-language-version": "<string: Minimum build SDK version required by dependencies, null means same as version>"
      },
      "moduleIdentifier": "<string: The unique identifier of the module in the build system, e.g. Gradle project path or Maven artifactId>",
      "testCommandWithCoverageTemplate": "<string: A concise shell command to calculate coverage by running existing tests + the coverage tool.
For multi-module Java (Maven/Gradle) projects where the project root has a build file
(e.g. a root pom.xml or settings.gradle), the command is executed from the project root
directory and must use -pl or task prefixes to target the module.
For standalone Java modules (no root build file, e.g. in a polyglot project), the command
is executed from the module directory and should NOT use -pl.
For Python, the command is executed from the module directory (where pyproject.toml
and .venv live).
The command must contain a placeholder {{filter}} representing the entire test filter clause
(flag + value), so that it can be removed to run all tests, or substituted with a
tool-specific filter expression.

  Ant with JaCoCo: "ant test-coverage {{filter}}"

import sqlite3
import tempfile
from pathlib import Path

import numpy as np
import pytest

from db import SQLiteBackend


@pytest.fixture
def backend():
    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir:
        db_path = Path(tmpdir) / "test.db"
        with SQLiteBackend(str(db_path)) as db:
            yield db


def test_insert_and_search(backend):
    embedding = np.random.rand(384).astype(np.float32)
    chunk_id = backend.insert_chunk(
        file_path="test.md",
        chunk_text="hello world",
        embedding=embedding,
        file_hash="abc123",
        updated_at="2026-05-14T00:00:00+05:30",
    )
    assert chunk_id == 1

    results = backend.search_hybrid(embedding, "hello", top_k=5)
    assert len(results) == 1
    assert results[0]["file_path"] == "test.md"


def test_delete_by_file(backend):
    emb = np.random.rand(384).astype(np.float32)
    backend.insert_chunk("a.md", "text a", emb, "h1", "2026-05-14")
    backend.insert_chunk("b.md", "text b", emb, "h2", "2026-05-14")

    backend.delete_by_file("a.md")
    results = backend.search_hybrid(emb, "text", top_k=10)
    assert len(results) == 1
    assert results[0]["file_path"] == "b.md"


def test_file_hash_roundtrip(backend):
    backend.set_file_hash("x.md", "hash1", "2026-05-14")
    assert backend.get_file_hash("x.md") == "hash1"

      "content" : "# Build Your AI Second Brain with Claude Code

A skill for [Claude Code](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code) that generates a personalized Product Requirements Document (PRD) for building your own AI second brain - a proactive, persistent AI assistant that knows your context, remembers across sessions, and keeps you informed.

**Watch the overview:** [How I Built My AI Second Brain with Claude Code](https://youtube.com/@ColeMedin) (YouTube)

## What This Does

You fill out a simple requirements template describing your tools, workflow, and preferences. Then Claude Code generates a phased build plan tailored to your setup - covering memory, integrations, skills, a proactive heartbeat, chat interface, and security hardening.

The generated PRD gives you (or your coding agent) a step-by-step blueprint for building a second brain that:

- **Remembers** across sessions - decisions, preferences, context, all in markdown files
- **Connects** to your platforms - Gmail, Slack, Calendar, Asana, Linear, GitHub, and more
- **Proactively monitors** your email, calendar, and tasks every 30 minutes
- **Chats** with you through Slack (or Discord, or any messaging platform)
- **Searches** months of memory with hybrid keyword + semantic search
- **Drafts** replies in your voice using RAG on your past messages
- **Tracks habits** with daily nudges inspired by Atomic Habits

## Quick Start

### 1. Install the Skill

Copy the `.claude/skills/create-second-brain-prd/` directory into your project:

```bash
# Clone this repo
git clone https://github.com/coleam00/second-brain-starter.git

# Copy the skill into your project
cp -r second-brain-starter/.claude/skills/create-second-brain-prd \
      your-project/.claude/skills/create-second-brain-prd
```

Or just copy the `.claude/` folder from this repo into an existing project.

### 2. Fill Out the Requirements Template

Copy the template to your workspace and fill it out:

```bash
cp .claude/skills/create-second-brain-prd/my-second-brain-requirements.md \
   ./my-second-brain-requirements.md
```

The template has 8 sections:

1. **About You** - Name, role, timezone
2. **Your Platforms** - Which tools you use (Gmail, Slack, Linear, etc.)
3. **Top Tasks** - 3-5 things you want AI to handle proactively
4. **Proactivity Level** - Observer, Advisor, Assistant, or Partner
5. **Security Boundaries** - What the agent should never do without permission
6. **Memory Categories** - What types of knowledge matter to you
7. **Infrastructure** - OS, local vs. VPS deployment
8. **Integration Priority** - Which 3 integrations to build first

See `example-my-second-brain-requirements.md` in the skill directory for a completed example.

### 3. Generate Your PRD

Open Claude Code in your project and run:

```
/create-second-brain-prd ./my-second-brain-requirements.md
```

Claude will:
1. Read your requirements
2. Load the architecture reference blueprint
3. Research every tool and API in your stack via web search
4. Generate a personalized 9-phase PRD at `.agent/plans/second-brain-prd.md`

### 4. Build It

Follow the phases in your PRD. Each phase includes:
- What to build and why
- Key files to create (with paths)
- Dependencies on previous phases
- Complexity estimate
- Personalization notes based on your answers

The recommended build order:

| Phase | What | Complexity |
|-------|------|------------|
| 1 | Memory Layer (SOUL.md, USER.md, MEMORY.md, daily logs) | Low |
| 2 | Hooks (SessionStart, PreCompact, SessionEnd) | Medium |
| 3 | Memory Search (hybrid keyword + semantic) | Medium |
| 4 | Integrations (your top 3 platforms) | Medium each |
| 5 | Skills (vault structure + custom skills) | Low-Medium |
| 6 | Proactive Systems (heartbeat + daily reflection) | High |
| 7 | Chat Interface (Slack/Discord bot) | High |
| 8 | Security Hardening (sanitization, guardrails) | Medium |
| 9 | Deployment (local scheduler or VPS) | Medium |

## Architecture Overview

The second brain is built on Claude Code and the Claude Agent SDK. No massive framework - just markdown files, Python scripts, and an Obsidian vault.

```
Memory Layer (center of everything)
    SOUL.md - Agent personality, values, boundaries
    USER.md - Your profile, accounts, preferences
    MEMORY.md - Key decisions, lessons, active projects
    daily/YYYY-MM-DD.md - Timestamped session logs

Hooks (context persistence)
    SessionStart - Loads memory into every conversation
    PreCompact - Saves context before auto-compaction
    SessionEnd - Captures decisions on exit

Integrations (platform connections)
    Python CLI wrapper pattern - LLM never sees API keys
    query.py gmail list / query.py asana overdue / etc.

Skills (extensible capabilities)
    Progressive disclosure - metadata always loaded, full instructions on demand

Heartbeat (proactive monitoring)
    Python gathers data -> Claude reasons -> notifications sent
    ~$0.05/run vs $0.38 with MCP tool calls

Memory Search (hybrid RAG)
    FastEmbed (local ONNX) + SQLite/Postgres
    70% vector + 30% keyword = best of both worlds
```

## Proactivity Levels

Your choice in the requirements template shapes the entire system:

| Level | What It Does |
|-------|-------------|
| **Observer** | Notifications only. Never takes action. |
| **Advisor** | Drafts emails/messages for your review. Tracks habits with suggestions. |
| **Assistant** | Auto-organizes files, auto-logs decisions. Asks for anything external. |
| **Partner** | Sends low-risk messages, completes routine tasks. Asks only for irreversible actions. |

- **Claude Agent SDK:** [Documentation](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/claude-code/sdk)
- **Obsidian:** [obsidian.md](https://obsidian.md)
